title: Smash Chicken Burger
tagline: Juicy coarse-mince chicken patty with a Greek-yogurt burger sauce.
servingsNote: 2 burgers, one thick patty each (or two thin smashed patties per burger).
macroNotes: Estimated for one burger: 200 g chicken thigh + brioche bun + 1 slice cheese + sauce + veg.
Thigh gives ~46 g protein and stays juicy; lean, well-trimmed thighs are closer to 650–680 kcal. Breast instead of thigh drops it to ~550 kcal / ~48 g protein with much less fat.
To cut calories: skip the cheese (−80 kcal), serve as a burger bowl over salad with no bun (−200 kcal, much leaner), or use the Greek-yogurt sauce instead of mayo (already built in).
The yogurt burger sauce is the high-protein swap — way better macros than a mayo-heavy sauce and it still tastes indulgent.

items: 400 g boneless skinless chicken thigh (juicier) — or breast for leaner
100 g Greek yogurt (or 30 g light mayo for a richer version)
1 tsp pickle juice (or a splash of vinegar)

steps.title: Coarse-mince the chicken
steps.body: Dice 400 g chicken thigh into chunks, then pulse in a food processor 5–8 times, or chop finely with a knife. Keep it a coarse mince — do NOT blend to a smooth paste; the texture is what gives a good bite.

steps.title: Season
steps.body: Mix in 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp pepper, 1 tsp paprika, 1 tsp garlic powder, 1 tsp onion powder, 1/2 tsp MSG, 1 tsp Dijon, 1 tsp soy sauce and 1 tbsp grated onion. Mix just until combined — don't overwork it or the patty turns rubbery. Thigh holds together without egg or breadcrumbs; if using lean breast, add 1 egg white as a binder.

steps.title: Shape and chill
steps.body: Divide into two balls. For classic patties, form two rounds about 10–11 cm wide and 1.5–2 cm thick. Chill 20–30 minutes if you can — it helps them hold together.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.body: Stir together 100 g Greek yogurt, 1 tsp Dijon, 1 tsp ketchup, 1 tsp pickle juice, a pinch of garlic powder and black pepper. Set aside.

steps.title: Cook (classic patty)
steps.body: Heat a pan over medium-high and add 1 tsp oil. Cook the patty 5–6 minutes on the first side without moving it, flip, and cook another 4–5 minutes to 74°C (165°F) in the center. Don't press down — you'll squeeze the juices out. Lay a slice of cheese on in the last minute and cover briefly to melt.

steps.title: Or smash it
steps.body: For crispy edges: divide into 80–100 g balls, drop onto a ripping-hot skillet and smash very thin with a spatula. Cook 2–3 minutes, flip once, add cheese, cook another 2 minutes. Use two smashed patties per burger — the browned Maillard edges add a lot of flavour.

steps.title: Toast the bun
steps.body: Toast the cut sides of 2 brioche buns in the same pan for 30–60 seconds to pick up the browned bits.

steps.title: Rest and assemble
steps.body: Let the patty rest 2–3 minutes. Build: bottom bun → sauce → lettuce → chicken patty → melted cheese → tomato → onion → pickles → a thin layer of sauce on the top bun. For a leaner burger-bowl, skip the bun and pile everything over a big salad of lettuce, cucumber, tomato, onion and cabbage.

notes: Thigh vs breast: thigh has intramuscular fat that keeps it juicy and forgiving, so it's the better burger cut even when well-trimmed. Breast is leaner but dries out fast — if using it, mix in a little soy/Worcestershire and don't overcook.
Optional crunch (+50–80 kcal): coat the patty lightly in seasoned panko or breadcrumbs, spray with oil and pan-fry or air-fry at 200°C for 10–12 min, flipping halfway.
Coarse mince, gentle mixing, no pressing while cooking, and a rest before assembly are the four things that keep it juicy.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
